---
title: "Unity-CLI Codex"
subtitle: "Driving the Unity Editor as a total, composable algebra"
---

This codex treats `unity-cli` not as a bag of commands but as a small algebra.
Every safe interaction with the Unity Editor is a composition of three
primitives, and every dangerous one is a primitive used out of order.

Pinned to `unity-cli` **v{{< unity-version >}}**.

## The three primitives

::: {.axiom title="Inspect → Mutate → Verify"}
A session is a sequence of effects bracketed by observation. You may not mutate
state you have not inspected, and you may not claim success for state you have
not verified.
:::

`Inspect` reads the world and is referentially transparent: running it twice
changes nothing. `Mutate` changes the world and must be bracketed. `Verify`
reads the world and answers a single yes/no question with no false greens.

| Primitive | Color | Nature | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inspect | cyan | pure, repeatable | `console`, `status`, an `exec` that only reads |
| Mutate | amber | effectful, bracketed | `editor play`, an `exec` that creates a GameObject |
| Verify | green | total, decisive | entity count, compile state, object-at-path |

## The composition law

Everything reduces to this bracket. Reparenting a baked entity, fixing a job
race, wiring an EntityLink — all of them are the same shape:

```{.cli name="The universal bracket"}
unity-cli status                 # Inspect: where am I, am I compiling?
unity-cli console --type error   # Inspect: is the board clean before I start?
# ... Mutate ...
unity-cli console --type error   # Verify: still clean?
# ... Verify the specific effect (entity count, object exists, etc.) ...
```

When a primitive is used out of order, the failure is structural, not
accidental. Creating an entity by hand instead of through baking is a `Mutate`
masquerading as a `Verify`: it makes the board look green without proving the
pipeline works.

::: {.failure title="Entity count zero is a hard failure"}
A subscene that bakes to zero entities is not "almost working." Do not paper
over it with manually-created entities or a passing test. Zero is red. Red is
not done.
:::

## What this codex is built from

The whole project is four orthogonal layers, each doing exactly one thing:

| Layer | Lives in | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Behavior | `_extensions/unity-cli/*.lua` | Render-time enforcement of the algebra |
| Presentation | `theme/*.scss` | Color-as-semantics, typography |
| Data | `data/types.yml` | The type registry |
| Content | `*.qmd` | This codex |

Delete any leaf and the rest still stands. The `exec` primitive below is
rendered by a Lua filter that also checks one invariant for you.

## A primitive renders itself

When you write an `exec` block, the behavior layer wraps it with its
invocation and checks that it is **total** — that it returns a value. A block
without a `return` is marked partial, because an `exec` that returns nothing
tells you nothing.

```{.exec name="Read the active scene" usings="UnityEditor.SceneManagement"}
var scene = EditorSceneManager.GetActiveScene();
return $"{scene.name} | {scene.path} | roots={scene.rootCount}";
```

Start with the [Axioms](axioms/algebra.qmd). Reach for the
[Recipes](recipes/runner.qmd) when you have a real task.
